flashcards.front: What is a dynamic array-based queue?
flashcards.back: A queue implemented using an array that automatically resizes (typically doubles) when it runs out of space, combining array efficiency with flexible capacity.

flashcards.front: What is amortized O(1) time for enqueue?
flashcards.back: Although a single enqueue may cost O(n) when resizing occurs, the average cost per enqueue over a sequence of n operations is O(1), because resizes happen infrequently.

flashcards.front: Why do dynamic arrays double rather than grow by a fixed constant?
flashcards.back: Doubling ensures that the total number of element copies across all resizes is proportional to n (geometric series), giving O(1) amortized cost. Growing by a constant leads to O(n) amortized cost per insertion.

flashcards.front: What is the role of realloc() in dynamic array queues?
flashcards.back: realloc() resizes an existing memory block. If the block can be extended in place, it does so; otherwise it allocates a new block, copies the data, and frees the old block.

flashcards.front: When should a dynamic array shrink?
flashcards.back: Shrink when size falls to capacity/4 (not capacity/2). This prevents thrashing -- repeated grow/shrink cycles caused by alternating insertions and deletions near the half-full boundary.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case space overhead of a doubling dynamic array?
flashcards.back: Up to 50% of allocated memory may be unused (immediately after a resize, the array is half full).
